locked_facts: Heat absorbed: Q = mcΔT (no phase change)
Heat for phase change: Q = mL (temperature stays constant during phase change)
Specific heat of water: c = 4186 J/kg·K (highest among common substances)
Latent heat of fusion of ice: L_f = 3.34 × 10⁵ J/kg
Latent heat of vaporisation of water: L_v = 2.26 × 10⁶ J/kg
Principle of calorimetry: heat lost = heat gained (in an isolated system)
During phase change, temperature remains constant even though heat is being added
truth_statements: The heat required to raise the temperature of a substance depends on its mass, specific heat capacity, and the temperature change
During a phase change the temperature remains constant because the heat energy is used to break intermolecular bonds rather than increase kinetic energy
Water has an unusually high specific heat capacity which moderates Earth's climate
The latent heat of vaporisation of water is about 7 times the latent heat of fusion because vaporisation requires completely breaking intermolecular bonds
In calorimetry experiments heat lost by the hot body equals heat gained by the cold body when the system is isolated

aha_moment.what_to_show: Temperature vs time graph for ice being heated to steam: flat plateau at 0°C (ice melting) and flat plateau at 100°C (water boiling). Between plateaus: temperature rises linearly. The plateaus show that heat goes into breaking bonds, NOT raising temperature.
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: Heating curve with clearly labelled plateaus at 0°C and 100°C — temperature-time graph with phases marked
aha_moment.why_this_breaks_misconception: Students think temperature always rises when you add heat — the plateaus during phase change are counterintuitive and crucial

simulation_states.label: Heating without phase change
simulation_states.description: Water heated from 20°C to 80°C. Q = mcΔT calculated. Temperature rises linearly with time (constant heating rate). Energy bar fills.
simulation_states.emphasis: Q = mcΔT — heat raises temperature when no phase change occurs

simulation_states.label: Phase change
simulation_states.description: Ice at -10°C heated continuously. Temperature rises to 0°C, then stays at 0°C while ice melts (Q = mL_f). After melting, temperature rises again.
simulation_states.emphasis: Temperature stays constant during melting — heat breaks bonds, doesn't increase KE

simulation_states.label: Complete heating curve
simulation_states.description: Ice (-20°C) → water → steam. Full heating curve with 5 segments: ice warming, ice melting, water warming, water boiling, steam warming. Two plateaus clearly visible.
simulation_states.emphasis: Two plateaus: melting and boiling. Heat goes in but temperature doesn't change.

simulation_states.label: Interactive
simulation_states.description: Student heats ice from -20°C with adjustable heating rate. Temperature-time graph builds. Calorimetry mixing: hot water + cold water → equilibrium temperature calculated.
simulation_states.emphasis: Watch the heating curve build. Mix hot and cold water — predict the final temperature.

common_misconceptions.belief: Temperature always increases when heat is added
common_misconceptions.correction: During a phase change (melting or boiling), temperature remains CONSTANT despite continuous heat input. The energy goes into breaking intermolecular bonds, not increasing molecular kinetic energy.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show heating curve with flat plateaus where heat is added but temperature doesn't change

common_misconceptions.belief: Steam at 100°C and water at 100°C have the same heat content
common_misconceptions.correction: Steam at 100°C has MUCH more energy than water at 100°C — the difference is L_v = 2.26 × 10⁶ J/kg. A steam burn is far worse than a hot water burn because the steam releases this latent heat on condensing.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show energy bars: water at 100°C vs steam at 100°C — steam has enormously more energy
